narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid but challenging background, we've carefully reviewed your law school application using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, based on patterns from thousands of past students. Your profile includes a Year 1 school decile of 4, a Year 3 decile of 3, lower-middle family income, an LSAT score of 23, an undergraduate GPA of 2.60, and plans to attend part-time while working. The model estimates a 51% probability that you would fail the bar exam initially, which is just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection—meaning the school prioritizes candidates predicted to pass confidently to ensure high success rates. The primary reason for this decision was your LSAT score, which stood out as the strongest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, especially since it's well below the typical student's score of around 37. Counterbalancing this somewhat were your family income level and early academic deciles, which nudged the prediction slightly toward passing compared to averages, suggesting your background and initial school performance showed some resilience. Your undergraduate GPA also leaned positive in influence, aligning closer to what's needed for success, while your later Year 3 decile and part-time status had smaller effects tilting toward higher risk. Overall, your metrics fell short of the stronger profiles—like higher test scores and full-time commitments—that typically predict bar passage for students like you. This isn't a reflection of your potential as a person, but a data-driven call to protect the program's outcomes. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly, strengthening your GPA through additional coursework, and exploring full-time options if feasible—these steps have turned around predictions for many in similar situations. You're clearly determined, and with targeted improvements, you could reapply stronger next cycle. We wish you the best in your journey toward law.
